# A01G Components, Pins, & Power (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Feb 2, 202611:59 PM EST

A **group** submission on GitHub Classroom & Gradescope.

**GitHub Classroom assignment:** [https://classroom.github.com/a/8ok2XwIn](https://classroom.github.com/a/8ok2XwIn) 

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8) 

Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

| Note: Please review the [Datasheets](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1WtkJ9VuYB_QBgVBrOpBYtgnW6IXIIFdv?usp=sharing) folder in Google Drive. Here, you’ll find the Silicon Labs wireless module datasheets and app notes that will assist you in this assignment and the rest of the semester. We have downloaded these for you, but they’re freely available on the manufacturer and distributor websites \- potentially, more updated versions\! |
| :---- |

# Learning Outcomes

* You will have verified that your chosen parts are in active development and can be purchased through a valid distributor.  
* You will create a bill of materials that captures the crucial details of your device parts.  
* Based on your component research, you will determine how much power your system needs to operate effectively \- allowing you to pick suitable voltage regulators.  
* You’ll have finalized your power architecture for your device, sorting out which voltage regulators to use by leveraging manufacturer simulation tools.  
* You will have determined what pins and protocols will be used to connect all parts to the microcontroller.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly. (Currently nothing)

# 1\. Component Verification & Simple BOM

An important part of engineering product design is to study and select components. However, we need to thoroughly check the part's lifecycle status, voltage range, and other critical characteristics to ensure it works in our design. Components in the market can be available one day and then gone the next. This is called **component stock** and it can vary wildly depending on the current state of the market.

Here are the critical characteristics you’ll be collecting in your Simple BOM:

* **Part Status** \- A part can be:  
  * **Active**: still actively being manufactured  
  * **Not Recommended for New Design (NRND)**: stock is available, but manufacturing will cease soon  
  * **Obsolete**: no manufacturing is taking place or only for existing customers with deep pockets\!  
  * If your part is not **Active**, you must find a new one.  
* **Available Stock** \- Number of components the distributor has.  
  * If your part stock is fewer than 100, you must find a new one.  
* **Manufacturer** \- the company that physically makes the part  
* **Manufacturer Part Number (MPN)** \- what the manufacturer calls their own part  
* **Distributor** \- the company who sells and ships the part  
* **Distributor Part Number** \- what the distributor calls the part in their internal system  
  * DigiKey or Mouser only for this class \- no other distributors are allowed  
  * You can search for the Adafruit & Sparkfun product numbers to find them on DigiKey  
* **Mounting type** \- how does the part attach to the printed circuit board (PCB)?  
  * Surface mount (SMT) components will be soldered by the PCBA manufacturer  
  * Through hole (THT) components will be soldered by hand by your team.  
* **Interface to MCU** \- how does the part communicate with the MCU?  
  * This could be any protocol, including I2C, SPI, UART, ADC, DAC, etc.  
* **Voltage range** \- the usable voltage range for the device  
  * Will inform the need for power supplies and potential logic level conversion  
* **Current consumption** \- how much amperage the part consumes best, worst, and quiescent cases  
* **Price** \- how much does the part cost in quantity 1 and quantity 1,000 (1k)?  
* **Dev board** \- what development board will you be using to evaluate your device before the PCBA is made?

Review the example tab in the [A01G BOM S26 - \[team name\]](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/16feUdgLOOsloCcX3yx-uKdlOmVqC9j4G_TXpFyeKmWk/edit?gid=619916013#gid=619916013) Google Sheet. Fill it in with the main components to be used by your project. Include the SiLabs 917 device, but there is **no need to include**:

* Power management ICs  
* Connectors  
* Passive components: resistors, capacitors, inductors.

**Note that this project requires the following components:**

* At least one component must use I2C communication  
* At least one sensor in your system  
* At least one actuator in your system

For each component, mention in the Excel spreadsheet what role it has in your system. Sensors, actuators, and the default processor require no more than mentioning it in the “Part Role” column. For miscellaneous components or other processors, ensure you justify where you would use it in the “Comments” column.

| You must add a link to a development board for your peripheral devices. If you find a component that has no dev board, you can either select a different component OR choose to [Solder your adapter board](#solder-your-adapter-board). |
| :---- |

## Solder your adapter board {#solder-your-adapter-board}

If you need to solder your own adapter board, find a suitable breakout adapter on [Proto Advantage](https://www.proto-advantage.com/store/). For example, the H bridge **DRV8833PWPR** has no dev board. However, we can find an adapter (to use a solderless breadboard) if we notice that the device’s footprint is a 16-pin TSSOP with a pitch of 0.65mm. You should format your search string like this: **\-pincount pitch adapter**. So, for instance this device adapter could be found with the following search query: ***\-16 0.65 tssop1***.

(1.1) Make a copy of the [A01G BOM - \[team name\]](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/16feUdgLOOsloCcX3yx-uKdlOmVqC9j4G_TXpFyeKmWk/edit?gid=619916013#gid=619916013) from the Class Drive folder into your own drive. Change the name to your team name and fill out the details for your project.  
Submit a link to your spreadsheet in your README.md  
Note: Make sure it is shared so graders can view your submission

# 2\. Power Requirements

Using the simple bill of materials from question 1, review what your power constraints are. There are some simple pie charts to guide your review, but you’ll need to determine **how much current you need per voltage supply**. If you have a 500mA motor on the 5V supply, that will not affect the 3.3V regulator sizing.

When doing power regulator sizing, it is important to add a safety margin to your numbers. For example, if I confidently determined that I needed 600mA of current for my 3.3V buck regulator, I would add 10% to that requirement. I would search for a 3.3V buck regulator that can supply ***at least*** 660mA of current.

| This section is meant to be a rough estimate of power. Silicon Labs has a neat tool \- [Simplicity Energy Profiler](https://docs.silabs.com/energy-profiler/latest/energy-profiler-start/) \- that can be run to get real, hardware power readings. We may use this later in the course. ![][image1] |
| :---- |

(2.1) In your BOM, fill it out as needed to determine the power requirements for the system. **Make sure to cite your sources** for the current values in your datasheet with the page number.

(2.2) In your README.md, write out the power requirements for all voltage regulators in your design.

# 

# 3\. Power Architecture

Now that we know how much power our system needs, it’s time to find voltage regulators for our system\! There are hundreds of thousands of power regulators in the market, and for this class, we’ve down-selected a few of our favorite power regulation ICs. Design your power architecture using the list of acceptable power regulation ICs [A01G Power Regulation Circuitry S26](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1p7kO2e1ty7Fl50hffwwKXHAY3KeLPKPKWQxLi_3JCPk/edit?gid=0#gid=0) and TI’s [Webench Circuit Designer](https://webench.ti.com/power-designer/).

Here’s a guide to using TI’s Webench: [Webench Sim Manual](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1JVmHDfpTvRm0_9qBnW7ZDUYq0SPH0x2K0iQsvu2w-EQ/edit?usp=sharing)

| Note: If your system needs a different regulation IC that’s not available on [A01G Power Regulation Circuitry S26](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1p7kO2e1ty7Fl50hffwwKXHAY3KeLPKPKWQxLi_3JCPk/edit?gid=0#gid=0),  you may research to find a suitable chip\! Feel free to reach out to your account manager and the teaching team for help.  |
| :---- |

(3.1) Create a block diagram of power architecture that details all the stages required for your system.  
Include the power block diagram in your README.md \- it should be clearly labeled and have the voltage and current characterization for associated converters

It is not required to capture the power connections to all non-power ICs in your circuit (Si917, sensors, actuators). You may review the example below as a starting point.

![][image2]

## Power Architecture Requirements & Notes

1. **Don’t forget to characterize the current requirements for your voltage regulators\!**

2. You are required to use a [Lithium Ion battery](https://www.adafruit.com/product/1781) and the [BQ24075](https://www.ti.com/product/BQ24075) as your power management IC.  
   Note the output of the BQ24075 system ranges from 3.3V to 5.25V depending on the power source\!

3. When using Webench, there seems to be an edge case for the minimum voltage input at 3.3V. Use \~3.4V as your Webench input to avoid this issue.  
   ![][image3]

4. Note the maximum current output of the BQ24075 is 4.5A \- if that’s not enough for your design, talk with the teaching team.

5. You are required to use at least one switching supply in your design.  
   This must be either a buck, boost, or buck-boost converter, designed using TI’s [Webench Circuit Designer](https://webench.ti.com/power-designer/). Your design will be saved on your TI account once you hit “Customize”. For each of the power regulator ICs, deliver the following:  
   * Schematics from TI Webench  
   * Input voltage range  
   * Output voltage  
   * Output current

   **Note: The BQ24075 (battery charging & power management IC) is exempt from the Webench simulation.**

(3.2) In your BOM, include your calculations and calculation results in the corresponding current columns. Link to your BOM for this submission.

(3.3) Include the screenshots of each Webench result \- we should be able to see the whole page, including the name of the selected component.

## Ripple Current and Voltage

For a refresher on switching regulators, refer to this TI Application Note: [Switching regulator fundamentals](https://www.ti.com/lit/an/snva559c/snva559c.pdf?ts=1737642181523)

Ripple current is a phenomenon associated with all types of switching converters. Buck, boost, and buck-boost converters (the three most common) operate by constantly charging and discharging an inductor, making the current oscillate by a little bit around the mean. As current oscillates, so too does the voltage. Generally, the ripple is small \- within tens of mV. However, it can vary significantly depending on the impedance of the rest of the circuit.

For some designs, ripple current can be a significant issue. Especially for sensitive components that require a very steady and noise-free voltage source, for example, the reference voltage of an ADC. For these sources, an LDO (Low Dropout Oscillator) is typically used. Ripple can be minimized by selecting appropriate values of inductance and output capacitance for your switching regulator.

In all designs, these are the questions to think about:

1. What components are sensitive to voltage ripple and what are not?  
   1. Some components, like a motor power supply, are not very sensitive to small variations in voltage levels. ICs generally are, and this can be dealt with by selecting the right values of capacitance on the line. And ensuring that decoupling capacitors are very close to the IC.  
2. How can I estimate the magnitude of ripple current and voltage?  
   1. The two major routes are (1) simulation on ECAD tools, like Webench, and (2) manual calculation, based on information in the datasheet (if given).  
   2. Generally, the exact value of the ripple is less important than the threshold \- if you can guarantee that your ripple is within a certain voltage/current range, it should be enough to make it work  
3. How do I protect the components that are sensitive to the ripple current?

(3.4) In your README.md, justify why the ripple current will or will not affect your board design and/or the components you’ve selected. Use the ripple calculator on Webench as well as your component datasheets to justify your answers.

# 

# 4\. MCU Pin & Protocol Assignment

Now that you have the power architecture and major components selected, it is time to determine the pinout of the SiWG917Y wireless microcontroller. **You must be very careful with this step. Please take time to double-check and verify that your pin assignment is correct\!** You may have to revise your BOM and other earlier sections as you work through the pin configuration \- make sure you give yourself time to change plans\!

## Pin Descriptions from Datasheets

![][image4]

Relevant datasheets (found in [Datasheets](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1WtkJ9VuYB_QBgVBrOpBYtgnW6IXIIFdv&usp=drive_fs)):

* [siw917x-family-rm REV1\_2.pdf](https://drive.google.com/open?id=13-t3d04PsfaUWDGpEd7wK9_sKgnwVVQj&usp=drive_fs): 1000+ page document relevant to all SiWG917 based designs  
* [siwg917y wireless module datasheet REV1\_1.pdf](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1Yxw2fW75UpK7Eu9T29dCBaa8qQqHQN_j&usp=drive_fs): \~200 page document relevant only to SiWG917 module designs  
* [siwg917-soc-module-errata REV0\_3.pdf](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1OMT6LhViBVaYgc3bg1-ev81eKIw0BZuW&usp=drive_fs): Document details issues present on various 917 SoC and module hardware

## 

## Pins that cannot be used in your design

The 917 module datasheet ([siwg917y wireless module datasheet REV1\_1.pdf](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1Yxw2fW75UpK7Eu9T29dCBaa8qQqHQN_j&usp=drive_fs)) calls out the few pins that are **not** available when using the wireless module (versus a chip down / IC design).  
![][image5]

## Pins recommended for your design

You can see the schematics and PCB layout for the BRD2708 dev board, which is our main development board. I’d recommend checking where these pins are routed \- and how easily accessible they are to you for testing.

* [BRD2708A-A03-schematic.pdf](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1kuxMjgKHDrm1lmTjXDDvKZLAgqyFNyK3&usp=drive_fs)  
* [PCB2708-A01-assy-draw.pdf](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1WVUX5-s37gLJ92uWP803AObVp4XrlgC0&usp=drive_fs)  
* [BRD2708 Altium PCB Source](https://upenn-eselabs.365.altium.com/designs/95ED483C-4607-4332-873B-66C3587954F6) 

| ![][image6] | ![][image7] |
| :---- | :---: |

## Pin & Protocol Assignment: Simplicity Studio 6 Pin Tool

Historically, pin assignment would require careful study of the microcontroller datasheet and manually assigning MCU and peripheral pin connections. This is still an excellent practice, but more and more microcontroller manufacturers are releasing software to aid in pin assignment. ST has [CubeMX](https://www.st.com/en/development-tools/stm32cubemx.html), Infineon has the [Pin & Code Wizard](https://www.infineon.com/cms/en/design-support/tools/utilities/fmx-pin-and-code-wizard/), Atmel/Microchip has [Atmel START](https://start.atmel.com/), and Silicon Labs has the [Simplicity Studio Pin Tool](https://docs.silabs.com/ssv6ug/latest/ssv6-configure-project/pin-tool). This software is useful, but not foolproof \- so you should review the datasheets to double-check.

To assign your pins, open [Simplicity Studio 6](https://docs.silabs.com/ssv6ug/latest/install-ssv6/install-simplicity-studio).

Make a **Virtual Device** of the [BRD2708A / EK2708 evaluation kit](https://www.silabs.com/development-tools/wireless/wi-fi/siw917y-ek2708a-explorer-kit). This is the main development board you’ll be using in this class. Creating a virtual device will allow you to load board hardware definitions that will make your pin selection simpler.  
![][image8]  
![][image9]

Click on the **Example Projects & Demos** tab, then search for **empty**. Choose one of the example **empty** projects (either C or C++, doesn’t matter). Continue through the pop-ups until the project is created.  
![][image10]  
![][image11]

Next, add elements you’ll require in your project through the **Software Components** tab of your project.  
For example, if I needed I2C support, I’d filter for I2C and install it. Hint: you can right click in the window and expand/collapse all of the submenus.  
![][image12]  
You can continue to configure these software settings by looking for little gear (![][image13]) icons.  
Here’s configuring the I2C instance.  
![][image14]  
Finally, if this exercise has changed any of the components on your BOM from earlier in the assignment, please update your BOM accordingly.  
After you’re configured the Silicon Labs Simplicity Studio project, you’ll see some files related to these configurations. The two most important ones for us right now are:

* **\*.pintool** \- an XML file that details the pin definitions from the pin assignment editor.  
  ![][image15]  
* **\*.slcp** \- an XML file that details the project configuration / software components included  
  ![][image16]

Commit this entire test project to your GitHub repository for this assignment.  
![][image17]

(4.1) Take a screenshot of all your software components and include them in your [README.md](http://README.md).  
Hint: in **Software Components**, you can check **Installed**, then right-click and select **Expand All** to see everything installed in your project.  
![][image18]

(4.2) In your BOM, put what pins were assigned to allow for communication from the MCU to the component. 

(4.3) Commit your Simplicity Studio generated project to your repository. The configuration files must detail pin and protocol assignments for all peripherals in your system.

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details.

For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
